beats.text: Training tells you that a network works, but the weights alone never tell you how it actually works.

visual.sub: From weights to a computation a person can audit

beats.text: Mechanistic interpretability recovers the algorithm itself, not which input pixels mattered as saliency maps ask.
The unit of explanation is the circuit, a subgraph of features and weights that together implement one identifiable function.
The program rests on three commitments: name the features, describe the circuit, and confirm each claim with a causal test.
A neuron is a fixed basis coordinate; a feature is a direction the network actually uses to store one concept.
Two facts make this hard: neurons rarely match concepts, and the cause is a deliberate encoding called superposition.
If features lined up with neurons, interpretability would be a labeling exercise: read each neuron, write the concept.

visual.sub: Why the natural basis is the wrong coordinate system

beats.text: A few neurons are cleanly monosemantic, firing for one thing, but most are polysemantic and fire for many.
One typical neuron can light up for French text, DNA codons, and HTTP headers, with no faithful single caption.
The linear representation assumption models an activation as a sparse non-negative sum of unit feature directions.

visual.note: EQ 13.1 — concepts are directions, and they add

beats.text: Training has no pressure to align features with axes, since rotating the hidden basis computes the same function.
Reading the neuron basis is like reading a paragraph with the word boundaries deleted: every letter present, meaning lost.
Superposition is the leading explanation: a network stores more features than dimensions using near-orthogonal directions.

visual.sub: Packing more features than there are dimensions

beats.text: With d dimensions you get only d orthogonal directions, but exponentially many nearly-orthogonal ones fit alongside them.
The price is interference: projecting onto a feature recovers its activation plus cross-talk from every overlapping feature.

visual.note: EQ 13.2 — cross-talk scales with pairwise cosine

beats.text: Superposition only works because features are sparse, so overlapping directions almost never fire on the same input.
The interference cost scales with the product of two firing probabilities, so sparse features make overpacking cheap.

visual.note: EQ 13.3 — sparser features, more you can pack

beats.text: As sparsity rises, toy networks leave one-feature-per-neuron and arrange features into pentagons and tetrahedra.

concepts.title: Sparse autoencoders: dictionary learning for features

beats.text: If superposition is the disease, dictionary learning is the cure, and a sparse autoencoder runs it at scale.

visual.sub: Re-expressing activations as a sparse learned dictionary

beats.text: The encoder maps an activation to a much wider code with a ReLU, then a decoder rebuilds it from feature directions.

visual.note: EQ 13.4 — the dictionary is overcomplete, 8x to 64x wider

beats.text: The loss balances two terms: reconstruct the activation faithfully, and keep the code sparse with an L1 penalty.

visual.note: EQ 13.5 — lambda sets the fidelity-versus-sparsity exchange rate

beats.text: L0 counts the nonzero entries in the code, so a code of 0.8, 1.2, and 0.4 active among eight has L0 of three.

visual.label: L0: active features in the code

beats.text: Three realities dominate practice: dead features, the width-versus-L0-versus-fidelity trilemma, and feature splitting.
Scaling Monosemanticity pushed SAEs onto Claude 3 Sonnet and found millions of locatable, manipulable features.
Features are nouns and circuits are the sentences: features wired by weights to actually compute something.
The best-traced example is the induction head, a two-head circuit that copies what followed an earlier matching token.
Two cheap tools dominate: the logit lens decodes intermediate layers, and ablation zeroes a component to test necessity.
Activation patching is the gold standard: transplant one location's activation from a corrupted run into a clean run.

visual.note: EQ 13.6 — a large delta means that location decides the answer

beats.text: If a clean logit of 2.1 becomes 6.3 after patching layer eight position five, the causal effect there is 4.2.
Patching is causal because it asks a counterfactual, which is stronger than ablation and far stronger than attention inspection.
Once a feature direction passes a causal test, you can steer by adding its decoder vector to the residual stream.

visual.sub: Writing concepts, and the limits of the method

beats.text: Adding alpha units of a feature direction pushes the model toward that concept, or clamping fixes its activation outright.

visual.tex: x' = x + \alpha\,\mathbf{d}_i \quad\text{or}\quad f_i(x) \leftarrow c \Rightarrow x' = x + (c - f_i(x))\,\mathbf{d}_i
visual.note: EQ 13.7 — the direction that reads a feature also writes it

beats.text: Clamping a Golden Gate Bridge feature high made the model fixate on the bridge across unrelated prompts.

visual.label: direction that both reads and writes

beats.text: Be honest: SAE features are not ground truth, since a different width or lambda yields a different feature set.
Evaluation is genuinely hard, reconstruction is incomplete, scaling is costly, and circuits remain mostly hand-found.
The honest scorecard: a working pipeline exists, but treat extracted features as falsifiable hypotheses, provisional always.
